title: Cheat Sheets
description: Concise revision prompts for LLMs, prompting, embeddings, RAG, agents, LLMOps, statistics, ML, and system design.

questions.title: Give a concise LLM fundamentals cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: You have 15 minutes before an LLM fundamentals screen.
questions.expectedAnswer: Know tokens, transformers, attention, context windows, pretraining, instruction tuning, RLHF or DPO, sampling, hallucinations, grounding, and model selection. Always connect model behavior to production risks: cost, latency, reliability, and evaluation.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Cheat Sheets?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise cheat sheets setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Give a concise RAG cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: You need a final review before a RAG design round.
questions.expectedAnswer: Remember ingestion, parsing, chunking, embeddings, indexing, retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, generation, citations, and evaluation. Key metrics include context recall, context precision, faithfulness, groundedness, answer relevance, and citation accuracy.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Cheat Sheets?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise cheat sheets setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Give a concise agents cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: You are preparing for an agent architecture discussion.
questions.expectedAnswer: Know agent versus workflow, tool calling, planning, memory, reflection, human review, safety, evaluation, and tracing. Use agents for open-ended dynamic tasks; use deterministic workflows when steps are known and reliability matters.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Cheat Sheets?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise cheat sheets setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Give a concise LLMOps cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: You need to explain how to operate a GenAI app after launch.
questions.expectedAnswer: Track prompt versions, model versions, datasets, evals, traces, costs, latency, safety events, user feedback, and deployment history. Use golden datasets, regression tests, observability, and rollback paths before production rollout.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Cheat Sheets?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise cheat sheets setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Give a concise statistics and ML cheat sheet.
questions.scenario: You are preparing for a mixed data science and ML engineering interview.
questions.expectedAnswer: Review p-values, confidence intervals, A/B tests, sampling bias, leakage, train-validation-test splits, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, overfitting, underfitting, feature engineering, and monitoring. Tie every metric to business impact.
commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for Cheat Sheets?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise cheat sheets setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: How would you explain prompt engineering revision in a Cheat Sheets interview?
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through prompt engineering revision for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define prompt engineering revision, explain why it matters for cheat sheets, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations.
